Scenario.title: Leviathan Communications Attack Scenario
Scenario.overview: Communications Complex An expert threat group targeted communication infrastructure through a multi-stage intrusion involving reconnaissance, resource development, and exploitation of various vulnerabilities to establish persistence, escalate privileges, and exfiltrate sensitive data while evading detection.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Logon Session, Script, Internet-facing assets, Command Prompt, Network Traffic, Application Log, OS Process, User Account, Credential Store, File, Target Artifact (Algorithm), Windows Service
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Logon Session Creation (Mechanism), Script Execution (Mechanism), Delivery Method, Command Execution (Mechanism), Application Log Content (Mechanism), Process Injection, User Account Authentication (Mechanism), Reg.exe Utility, Pass the Hash, File Modification (Mechanism), Encrypted Channel, Service Execution

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: An adversary initiated reconnaissance by creating or manipulating logon sessions to gather OSINT on the target's communication infrastructure, specifically focusing on SharePoint-based systems to collect publicly accessible data like emails or documents.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The attacker developed automated collection scripts to harvest data from communication protocols, tailoring them to blend with normal traffic and avoid suspicion during the setup phase.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2022-22674: macOS Monterey Out-of-Bounds Read Vulnerability
stage.description: Exploiting remote services on internet-facing assets, the adversary leveraged a suggested CVE-2022-22674 to compromise systems via blocked or manipulated access points, gaining entry through methods like phishing or credential stuffing targeting communication software.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2019-16920: D-Link Router Command Injection Vulnerability
stage.capec: CAPEC-9: Buffer Overflow in Local Command-Line Utilities
stage.description: Upon gaining initial access, the attacker executed indirect commands through vulnerable mechanisms, such as command injection in D-Link routers (CVE-2019-16920), to run malicious payloads that intercept and alter communication data flows.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: To maintain long-term access, the adversary established persistence by poisoning DNS caches (CAPEC-142), ensuring ongoing manipulation of communication routes and allowing re-entry after disruptions.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Elevating privileges, the attacker exploited weaknesses in application logs to gain administrative control, enabling deeper modifications to communication systems for enhanced espionage capabilities.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Faking legitimate activities, the adversary injected malicious processes into OS-level communications to evade detection, blending with normal operations like fake antivirus scans.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: Strengthening access, the attacker stole credentials through password guessing or recovery exploits, compromising user accounts on communication platforms to monitor and escalate further unauthorized activities.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Scanning for active communication endpoints, the adversary used network sniffing techniques to discover internal services and vulnerabilities, particularly focusing on credential stores accessed via reg.exe utilities.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: Moving across the network, the attacker propagated access using pass-the-hash methods on compromised credentials, expanding control over multiple communication devices undetected.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Harvesting sensitive data, the adversary collected communication artifacts like emails and chat logs by modifying files or exploiting data interchange protocols for destruction or retrieval purposes.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Transferring data covertly, the attacker exfiltrated collected information through encrypted channels like HTTPS or DNS tunneling to obscure the activity from monitoring systems.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Inflating damage, the adversary caused significant impacts by executing services to launch DoS attacks, destroy critical files, or send fraudulent messages overwhelming communication systems.

Scenario.summary: The Leviathan Australian Intrusions campaign demonstrated expert-level sophistication by targeting communication infrastructure with a complex attack flow, resulting in extensive espionage, disruption, and data theft.
